title: Sharpening: Enhancing Edges
concept: Sharpening filters exaggerate the difference between a pixel and its neighbors, boosting contrast at edges — the mirror opposite of blurring.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a blurring filter do to a pixel's value?

options.text: Averages it with its nearby neighbors, smoothing out differences
Multiplies it by the image width
Deletes it if it's too bright

exercises.explain: Right — blurring averages neighbors together. Sharpening is about to do the opposite.

cards.title: The "Enhance" button
cards.body: A slightly soft photo suddenly looks crisp after tapping "Sharpen." Edges that were fuzzy now look defined, textures pop. What's happening isn't adding new detail — it's amplifying differences that were already faintly there.

cards.title: What is sharpening?
cards.body: Sharpening is a filter that exaggerates the difference between a pixel and its neighbors. Where blurring pulls values toward their neighborhood's average, sharpening pushes values further apart — making edges look more defined.

cards.title: Turning up the contrast dial
cards.body: Think of two people speaking at almost the same volume — hard to tell them apart. Sharpening is like making the louder one louder and the quieter one quieter: small differences become easy to notice.

cards.title: Crisping up a slightly soft photo
cards.body: A photo that's just a touch out of focus has edges that fade gradually instead of jumping sharply. A sharpening filter looks at each pixel, compares it to its neighbors, and pushes the boundary to look more sudden — like tracing over a faint pencil line with a pen.

cards.title: Sharpening can't recover lost detail
cards.body: A common myth: sharpening can "un-blur" a photo and bring back detail that's truly gone. It can't — it only emphasizes differences that still exist, even faintly. Push it too hard on a very blurry photo and you just get ugly halos around edges, not real detail.

left.text:
Shrinks differences between neighbors
Result: smoother, softer image

right.text:
Grows differences between neighbors
Result: more defined, crisper edges

exercises.prompt: What does a sharpening filter do compared to blurring?

options.text: It exaggerates the differences between a pixel and its neighbors, instead of averaging them away
It also averages neighbors, just with a smaller window
It deletes weak edges from the photo
It converts the photo to grayscale

exercises.explain: Exactly — sharpening is the mirror opposite of blurring: it grows differences instead of shrinking them.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Sharpening can restore detail that was completely lost to heavy blurring.
exercises.explain: False. Sharpening can only emphasize differences that still faintly exist — it can't invent detail that's truly gone.

exercises.sentence: While blurring shrinks differences between neighbors, sharpening ___ them.
exercises.explain: Exaggerates is correct — sharpening pushes neighboring pixel differences further apart.

exercises.prompt: Which result shows a sharpening filter at work?

options.text: A slightly soft photo now shows crisper, more defined edges around objects
A noisy photo now looks smoother with less graininess
A photo's file size shrinks after saving

exercises.explain: Crisper, more defined edges are the signature effect of a sharpening filter.

exercises.prompt: You sharpen an already very blurry, out-of-focus photo as hard as possible, hoping to restore lost detail. What actually happens?

options.text: The photo gets ugly halos and exaggerated noise around edges, but the missing detail never truly comes back
The photo becomes exactly as sharp as the original, undoing the blur completely
Sharpening automatically blurs the photo back to its blurry state
Nothing changes, because sharpening only works on already-sharp photos

exercises.explain: Right — sharpening amplifies whatever differences remain; it can't recreate detail that blurring erased. Next: what if we want a filter built specifically to find edges, not just enhance them?
